title: Is Netflix Stock a Buy, Hold, or Sell in March?
content:
With its shares already down roughly 28% from an all-time high of $134 reached in mid-June, Netflix (NFLX +0.53%) has faced significant challenges over the past few months. The situation came to a head in late 2025 when its management revealed plans to acquire the studio and streaming assets of Warner Bros -- a move some analysts feared could undermine shareholder value by loading Netflix's balance sheet with debt.

However, in late February, Netflix withdrew its bid for Warner Bros, allowing skeptical investors to rest a little easier. Let's explore what this stunning reversal might mean for the company's stock in March and beyond.

It looked like a done deal. On Dec. 5, Netflix announced that it would acquire Warner Bros. for a total enterprise value of $82.7 billion (including taking on its debt). The acquisition would have given the combined company access to Warner Bros.' iconic intellectual properties like Harry Potter, the DC Comic Universe, and Game of Thrones, along with the tools to monetize them more effectively through Netflix's streaming empire.

But despite the clear synergies, the market reacted extremely negatively to the proposal, sending Netflix shares down double-digits and prompting analyst downgrades. It's easy to see why investors were skeptical.

According to research from Fortune magazine, a whopping 70%-75% of acquisitions fail to create shareholder value for the acquiring company. And with a target as large as Warner Bros., the stakes were quite high. Even if operational results improved, it could take years or even decades to defray the cash and debt used to make the deal. Investors are comparing these prospects to other, less risky uses of the capital, such as buybacks, which could have a bigger impact on stock performance.

With all that in mind, the market reacted positively this week when Paramount Skydance raised the stakes by offering $110 billion for Warner Bros. Netflix had the right to make a higher counteroffer; however, management decided to withdraw instead, saying the deal was no longer financially attractive enough for it to pursue.

Right now, it looks likely that Paramount Skydance will eventually merge with the studio and streaming assets of Warner Bros. This acquisition could make Paramount a much more serious competitor in the video streaming market by giving it access to a library of well-known intellectual property assets and more robust film and television production capacity through its vast network of movie studios and industry connections.

For Netflix, this could mean that customers have more alternatives for high-quality streaming subscriptions. That said, Paramount and Warner Bros are already competing with Netflix as separate companies. And it remains unclear how the combination will change the overall dynamics.

The Wall Street Journal reports that Netflix may actually be the winner in this situation because it will receive a $2.8 billion termination fee while avoiding complicating its business model. Most importantly, now that the streaming giant has backed out of the acquisition deal, investors can refocus on its strong fundamentals and long-term growth drivers.

Going forward, Netflix's stock performance will likely depend on its earnings. The numbers remain encouraging, with first-quarter revenue forecast to jump 15.3% year over year to $12.2 billion, with an expected operating income of $3.9 billion (up 17% from the prior-year period).

While the company is likely near saturation in developed markets like North America and Western Europe, it can still drive continued top and bottom line growth with monetization strategies like advertising. Advertising represented a modest $1.5 billion in 2025 revenue, but management expects this to double to $3 billion in 2026. And over the coming years, it will likely become one of Netflix's core revenue streams.

Now that the controversial acquisition is off the table, Netflix's recent stock price declines look like a good buying opportunity for long-term investors.

*Average returns of all recommendations since inception. Cost basis and return based on previous market day close.

title: Netflix Abandons Warner Deal To Double Down On Content And Partnerships
content:
Never miss an important update on your stock portfolio and cut through the noise. Over 7 million investors trust Simply Wall St to stay informed where it matters for FREE.

Netflix (NasdaqGS:NFLX) has walked away from its planned acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery, triggering a sizeable breakup fee in Netflix's favor.

The company plans to channel this additional cash into organic content spending and new partnerships with traditional broadcasters.

The decision reflects Netflix's focus on its core streaming platform instead of large scale mergers and acquisitions.

Netflix shares trade at $98.66, with the stock up 16.6% over the past week and 23.4% over the past month. Over the past 3 years, the share price has risen 231.3%, and 88.6% over 5 years, which highlights how central the company has become to the streaming market. The current move away from a large acquisition puts the spotlight back on how management allocates capital within the existing business.

The breakup fee and renewed focus on content and broadcaster deals could influence how Netflix shapes its programming slate and distribution reach. For investors, the key questions now revolve around how efficiently the company can turn this extra financial flexibility and partnership activity into viewer engagement and, over time, earnings power.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Netflix by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Netflix.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 2 things going right for Netflix that every investor should see.

Netflix walking away from Warner Bros. Discovery looks like a clear choice to protect its balance sheet and keep flexibility, rather than take on a huge integration project and potential regulatory hurdles. Instead of paying up for Harry Potter or Game of Thrones outright, Netflix is leaning into what has worked so far: heavy content spending, ad-tier growth, and a bigger role as a distribution hub for broadcasters such as the BBC and TF1. That approach keeps fixed obligations lower than a mega deal and may help the company respond faster if viewing habits or pricing pressure shift. At the same time, rivals like Paramount Skydance, Disney, and Amazon are pursuing scale through tie ups and bundled platforms, which could crowd the market with larger content libraries. The US$2.8b breakup fee and planned US$20b content spend give Netflix room to test areas like live sports, video podcasting, and international news partnerships without being tied to one legacy studio. For you as an investor, the question is whether this builder not buyer stance leaves Netflix with the right mix of owned content, licensed franchises, and partner channels compared with those more consolidated peers.

The decision to walk away from Warner Bros. and redirect a US$2.8b breakup fee into content and partnerships fits the narrative of relying on ad tech, localized content, and AI-powered personalization to drive engagement rather than buying growth.

The original narrative assumed Warner assets could expand content scale, so Netflix stepping back tests the view that organic content investment and broadcaster deals alone can sustain the long term growth story.

The push to host traditional broadcasters directly on the platform, and potential moves in areas like live sports and video podcasting, is not deeply reflected in the earlier focus on ad tech and international originals and could become a bigger part of the story over time.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story.  Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Netflix to help decide what it's worth to you.

⚠️ Walking away from Warner Bros. means Netflix misses out on owning major franchises like Harry Potter and Game of Thrones at a time when Disney and Paramount Skydance are using deep libraries to keep viewers in their own ecosystems.

⚠️ Management is leaning harder on execution around US$20b of annual content spend and new areas such as live sports and advertising, so any missteps could weaken returns on that capital and leave the company exposed to rivals like Amazon Prime Video and Disney+.

🎁 Avoiding an US$80b plus studio deal and associated debt helps preserve balance sheet flexibility and reduces integration and regulatory risks that can weigh on cash flow for years.

🎁 The breakup fee and focus on broadcaster partnerships, ad tier monetization, and international content give Netflix multiple levers to pursue growth without being tied to a single large acquisition.

From here, it makes sense to watch how quickly Netflix deploys the US$2.8b breakup fee and broader cash flow into new content and broadcaster deals, and whether that shows up in subscriber engagement and ad revenue. Keep an eye on how the combined Paramount Skydance and Warner Bros. unit positions its merged HBO Max and Paramount+ service, including pricing and content release cadence, because that will shape how crowded the premium streaming space feels. It is also worth tracking commentary from the upcoming Morgan Stanley technology, media and telecom conference, where Netflix’s CFO is expected to speak about capital allocation and the outlook for content spending and share repurchases.

To ensure you're always in the loop on how the latest news impacts the investment narrative for Netflix, head to the  community page for Netflix to never miss an update on the top community narratives.

title: Live Nasdaq Composite: Tech Stocks Ride Catalysts in Mixed Showing
content:
Apple (AAPL) and Nvidia (NVDA) fell, Netflix (NFLX) and Broadcom (AVGO) rose (AVGO $2.05 EPS), CNX Resources (CNX) near highs, Berkshire (BRK-B) CEO buying $15M, Morgan Stanley (MS) cuts 2,500 jobs, Trade Desk (TTD) up 23%, CEO bought $6M.

Markets fell on fearful sentiment amid mixed earnings and corporate actions including Broadcom’s beat, Berkshire CEO’s stock purchase commitment, and Morgan Stanley layoffs.

Netflix may have bowed out of the Warner Bros bidding, but it’s making another deal. The content streaming giant has acquired AI filmmaking platform InterPositive, whose founder is famous actor and filmmaker Ben Affleck. NFLX stock is currently up fractionally.

Nvidia has stopped production of its H2oo chips that were headed for China’s market and has instead pivoted to its Vera Rubin AI chip set, directing production capacity from Taiwan’s TSMC toward the more efficient chip solution, according to the FT. Nvidia stock is now fractionally higher. The Nasdaq Composite has turned lower.

Fewer Americans filed for unemployment benefits last week than economists expected, with initial jobless claims totaling 213,000 vs. forecasts calling for 215,000 as the labor market continues to signal stability.

This article will be updated throughout the day, so check back often for more daily updates.

Fearful sentiment has returned, with all three of the major stock market averages losing ground out of the gate. The Nasdaq Composite is down fractionally amid a mixed showing, with Apple (Nasdaq: AAPL | AAPL Price Prediction) and Nvidia (Nasdaq: NVDA) under pressure while Netflix (Nasdaq: NFLX) and Broadcom (Nasdaq: AVGO) are bucking the downward trend and moving higher. Semiconductor stocks are lower as a group. Crude oil is trading at nearly $78 per barrel, while Brent Crude is hovering just below $84/barrel. Natural gas stock CNX Resources (NYSE: CNX) is nearing 52-week high levels.

Bank of America warns that a spike in oil prices remains the primary threat to the U.S. economy, noting that the U.S.-Iran war has done little to change the broader economic outlook. For now, risks remain contained unless crude prices take a significant leg higher.

Here’s a look at where things stand as of morning trading:

Dow Jones Industrial Average: 48,418.43 Down 326.36 (-0.67%)
Nasdaq Composite: 22,763.88 Down 18.27 (-0.27%)
S&P 500: 6,849.65 Down 20.68 (-0.30%)

Broadcom stock is rising in response to its latest earnings report. The company’s Q1 EPS of $2.05 per share beat analyst estimates. Wall Street analysts are turning increasingly bullish on AVGO for its AI and data center positioning.

New Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE: BRK-B) CEO Greg Abel has shared plans to reportedly direct his entire paycheck to buying more BRK stock for as long as he is leading the company, according to reports. Abel officially took the helm from Warren Buffett in recent days. To kick things off, he’s  so far filed to purchase $15 million worth of Berkshire shares.

Investment bank Morgan Stanley (NYSE: MS) is implementing cost-cutting firmwide, slashing 2,500 jobs across divisions or 3% of its global workforce.

The Trade Desk (Nasdaq: TTD) is soaring by over 23% as investors buy the dip after an earnings-triggered sell-off that had sent the stock spiraling in recent days. The CEO has recently went on a buying spree, acquiring $6 million worth of TTD shares for his portfolio.

title: My Top Semiconductor Pick Rose 49% in 2025. Is It Still a Buy in 2026?
content:
Semiconductors have been and remain one of the biggest AI-driven success stories in the market.

Big growth is expected in 2026, but Nvidia's post-earnings price drop suggests that growth rate is fully priced in.

The VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) is still lining up for further gains in 2026.

10 stocks we like better than VanEck ETF Trust - VanEck Semiconductor ETF ›

The artificial intelligence (AI) trade has been completely upended in 2026.

Last year, investors focused on the immense potential of AI and how it could transform businesses and the global economy. This year, markets are trying to assess who the winners and losers will be in the AI boom. And the latter group is getting hit hard.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »

This is a popular chart that's been making the rounds. It compares the relative performance of software stocks to semiconductor stocks.

Fundamental Chart data by YCharts

The implication is pretty clear. Investors believe that the demand for chips (and the subsequent revenue and earnings growth that comes from it) is likely to continue. The software sector, on the other hand, is thought to be replaceable by AI as coding capabilities continue to improve.

For the time being, it looks as if semiconductors are the way investors will play AI growth.

My top pick for this space was the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ: SMH). Its concentrated exposure in this sector (it owns around 25 names and market cap-weights them) helped produce a 49% return in 2025. It's up another 12% in 2026 year to date.

Clearly, investors love their semiconductor stocks, but can it sustain its momentum?

There's no question that the AI boom will fuel chip demand for the foreseeable future. The global semiconductor sector is expected to hit $975 billion in sales in 2026, a 26% growth rate compared to the previous year.

That growth rate will start to slow eventually. Over the next year, however, 26% growth looks like a reachable target. Given big tech company spending on AI buildouts and the number of years it's likely to take to get close to anything resembling a finished product, strong growth is likely to keep fueling stock price gains.

The biggest question is whether investors are already paying too much for this growth story.

The VanEck Semiconductor ETF currently trades at 45 times trailing 12-month earnings. If we assume that 26% growth rate, the forward price/earnings (P/E) ratio gets closer to 35.

Anytime the P/E ratio gets significantly above the growth rate, the value question comes into play. We just saw Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) report quarterly revenue expectations, earnings expectations, and included raised guidance for the coming year. The stock still lost more than 5% on the day following the announcement.

That suggests the market may have fully priced growth expectations into stock prices. And any miss in the near future could be met with a harsh reaction in the stock price.

Overall, I still like the outlook for the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. One risk, however, is how top-heavy it is. Roughly 30% of the portfolio is dedicated to the combination of Nvidia and Taiwan Semiconductor. That will make future performance heavily dependent on just two companies.

But the market is still sticking with the industry's mega-cap leaders for the time being. As long as that's the case, this ETF could be lined up for another strong year in 2026.

title: Why I'm Watching SentinelOne Stock Heading Into March
content:
SentinelOne stock has struggled over the past year.

The AI-native cybersecurity company has some unique advantages and just rolled out a new product.

Will rising geopolitical tensions increase the demand for cybersecurity?

10 stocks we like better than SentinelOne ›

March should be an interesting month for one stock in particular, SentinelOne (NYSE: S).

The cybersecurity stock has struggled since it went public nearly five years ago. Despite an initial jolt higher during the 2021 tech boom when it surpassed $70 per share, it has steadily declined since, down to its current $13.50 per share price.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »

Over the past 12 months, it has dropped 32%, and year to date it is off 10%.

While SentinelOne has had robust revenue growth, rising 23% in the last quarter, its pace of growth is expected to slow in fiscal 2026. Last fiscal year revenue increased 32%, but this fiscal year, which ended Jan. 31, revenue is projected to rise only 21%. That is lower than initially guided for.

But this month is an interesting one for SentinelOne and could potentially be a turning point.

Over the past couple of weeks, SentinelOne stock has surged about 9% higher, based on a couple of catalysts. The initial catalyst is the rollout of a new platform to thwart identity attacks.

The platform is an expansion and improvement of its identity security offerings. It protects not just human identities, but also those of non-human agentic artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots. In addition, its protection goes beyond the gateway, or log-in, and provides continuous runtime validation, meaning it monitors the user throughout the session.

While other competitors offer this service, SentinelOne is one of a few that does so on an AI-native platform, meaning the platform was originally built for AI algorithms. Other much larger competitors bolted on AI capabilities and require the cloud to handle threats.

AI native means that the AI brain is built into the device and doesn't rely solely on the cloud, as is the case with many others. That enables quicker and more efficient decisions as the AI-native capability can eliminate threats directly on the device, as opposed to going through the cloud. Also, it works when the device is offline, not just when it's connected to the cloud.

It will be interesting to see if this new offering can boost revenue and make a dent in the market share of leaders like CrowdStrike in endpoint security and Okta and Microsoft in identity protection, among others.

Another reason could be the current geopolitical tensions between Iran and the U.S. Typically, at a time of war or geopolitical unrest, cybersecurity stocks get a boost because investors see them as more in demand as companies, organizations, and government agencies shore up their cyber defenses against potential attacks.

This all comes as SentinelOne gets ready to report its fiscal fourth-quarter and year-end earnings on Thursday, March 12. I'll be watching that closely to see if SentinelOne exceeds or falls short of expectations.

But more importantly, I'll be watching to see what the outlook for the next fiscal year is. It could indicate if this new product, its AI-native advantages, and geopolitical tensions all increase demand for SentinelOne.
